title: GDPR Consent Management Audit Trail

compliance.assurance_rationale: This UC supplies two of the three evidentiary primitives Art.7 demands of a controller. (a) For Art.7(1) demonstration of consent: each grant event carries the IAB TCF v2.2 `consent_string` (which encodes the purposes, the Global Vendor List version, and the publisher purposes the data subject was shown) plus the CMP banner `ui_version` (which links to the legal text version that was on screen). The combination is replayable — a controller can show the regulator exactly what was put to a subject at grant time. (b) For Art.7(3) withdrawal-as-easy-as-grant: the per-purpose 7-day rolling withdrawal-to-grant ratio quantifies parity; on a stable population of >1000 grants per purpose, a ratio under 5% is a statistically meaningful red flag of an asymmetric flow (the metric is the same shape EDPB Guidelines 05/2020 §113–115 use to evaluate parity). The UC does NOT prove Art.7(2) intelligibility (which needs human review of the banner text) or Art.7(4) freely-given (which needs banner-display-to-grant timing analysis to detect cookie-wall and accept-to-read patterns; deferred to UC-22.1.6). Hence partial, not full.
compliance.controlObjective: Per-purpose evidence that every grant carries a parseable TCF v2.2 consent_string and ui_version (Art.7(1) demonstration), and that the rolling withdrawal-to-grant ratio sits inside the privacy-office-signed band evidencing withdrawal-as-easy-as-grant parity (Art.7(3)).
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Per-purpose row in audit_evidence (7y retention, privacy_office ACL): purpose_id, grants, withdrawals, ratio, grants_missing_consent_string, art7_1_evidence_gap, art7_3_parity_gap. Plus raw CMP records (subject_id_hash, action, purpose_id, tcf_version, consent_string, ui_version, geo, ts_grant/ts_withdraw) in the consent index. Weekly CSV digest to ServiceNow GRC for DPIA review.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Derived from GDPR@2016/679 Art.7 (parent assurance='partial'). UK GDPR preserves parent clause numbering; inherited assurance degraded one step.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that UK GDPR Art.7 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.5: GDPR Consent Management Audit Trail.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.5' running on sourcetype cmp:tcf:event and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: Two complementary sources are joined. (1) **CMP webhook events via HEC** at `index=consent sourcetype="cmp:tcf:event"` — structured JSON emitted by an IAB TCF v2.2-compliant Consent Management Platform (tested on OneTrust, TrustArc, Didomi, Cookiebot, Iubenda) carrying `subject_id` (pseudonymous hash), `action` (`grant` | `withdraw` | `update`), `purpose_id` (IAB TCF v2.2 purpose 1–11), `purpose_name`, `legal_basis`, `consent_string` (TCF v2.2 base64url), `tcf_version`, `ui_version` (banner build code), `geo`, `gdpr_applies`, `ts`. (2) **Apache and nginx access logs** at `index=web sourcetype="access_combined"` (and `sourcetype="nginx:access"` if applicable) — every HTTP-level interaction with the consent UI: GETs of the banner URI, POSTs to the consent and withdrawal endpoints. The CMP ledger is authoritative for payload; the web logs are the corroborating fingerprint that survives a CMP webhook outage.
app: `Splunk_TA_apache` ([Splunkbase 3186](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/3186)), Splunk-supported, deployed to Universal Forwarders on the web tier and to every Search Head consuming the search. Splunk **HTTP Event Collector** (HEC) — platform capability of Splunk Enterprise 9.x and Splunk Cloud, configured via Settings → Data Inputs → HTTP Event Collector with a dedicated `cmp_tcf_webhook` token scoped to `index=consent` and sourcetype `cmp:tcf:event`. nginx (where present) ingested either via Universal Forwarder file inputs with a private `props.conf` for `nginx:access` OR via the community `TA-nginx` ([Splunkbase 3258](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/3258); community-supported, NOT Splunk-supported — fold its props.conf into a private TA if compliance requires Splunk-supported add-ons only).
spl:
(index=consent sourcetype="cmp:tcf:event" earliest=-7d@d)
OR (index=web sourcetype="access_combined" earliest=-7d@d (uri="/cmp/banner" OR uri="/api/consent" OR uri="/cmp/withdraw"))
| eval action = case(
    sourcetype=="cmp:tcf:event", action,
    method=="POST" AND like(uri,"/api/consent%"), "grant",
    method=="POST" AND like(uri,"/cmp/withdraw%"), "withdraw",
    1=1, "view"
  )
| where action IN ("grant","withdraw")
| where coalesce(gdpr_applies,"true")=="true"
| eval purpose_id = coalesce(purpose_id, "unspecified")
| eval consent_string_ok = case(
    action=="withdraw", 1,
    action=="grant" AND match(coalesce(consent_string,""), "^C[A-Za-z0-9_-]{20,}$") AND tcf_version=="2.2", 1,
    1=1, 0
  )
| eval ui_version_ok = if(isnotnull(ui_version) AND ui_version!="", 1, 0)
| stats count(eval(action=="grant")) as grants
        count(eval(action=="withdraw")) as withdrawals
        sum(eval(if(action=="grant" AND consent_string_ok==0, 1, 0))) as grants_missing_consent_string
        sum(eval(if(action=="grant" AND ui_version_ok==0, 1, 0))) as grants_missing_ui_version
        by purpose_id
| eval withdrawal_to_grant_ratio = round(withdrawals / if(grants>0, grants, 1), 4)
| eval art7_1_evidence_gap = if(grants_missing_consent_string>0 OR grants_missing_ui_version>0, "yes", "no")
| eval art7_3_parity_gap = if(grants>1000 AND withdrawal_to_grant_ratio<0.05, "yes", "no")
| where art7_1_evidence_gap="yes" OR art7_3_parity_gap="yes"
| sort - grants
description: Detects gaps in the GDPR Article 7 evidence chain by joining web-server consent endpoint hits with structured Consent Management Platform (CMP) events and flagging two failure modes per IAB TCF v2.2 purpose: (a) grant events recorded without a parseable TCF consent_string or `ui_version` — the controller cannot demonstrate to a regulator what terms were shown to the data subject (Art.7(1)); and (b) populations with more than 1000 grants per purpose where the rolling 7-day withdrawal-to-grant ratio falls below 5%, indicating that the withdrawal flow is materially harder than the grant flow (Art.7(3) parity gap).
value: Article 7 is the operational hinge of every EU consumer-facing app: a single failed regulator demonstration of consent under Art.7(1), or a finding that withdrawal is harder than grant under Art.7(3), is the path most often cited in supervisory-authority enforcement letters and class-action statements of claim. CNIL's January 2023 TikTok cookie-consent decision (deliberation SAN-2022-026, EUR 5m) is the canonical case study — it turned not on a missing CMP but on the CMP's withdrawal flow being demonstrably harder than the grant flow under inspection. This UC turns the CMP's own ledger into a Splunk-side audit trail the controller can replay without depending on the CMP vendor's user interface, with a 7-year-retention restricted index ready for regulator inspection on demand.
implementation: Stand up a dedicated `consent` index, mint a HEC token scoped to it, point the CMP webhook (OneTrust / TrustArc / Didomi / Cookiebot / Iubenda) at the HEC endpoint with the TCF v2.2 payload enabled, and deploy `Splunk_TA_apache` to the web tier so consent endpoint hits land in `index=web`. Restrict both indexes to a `privacy_office` role using `srchIndexesAllowed` and a purpose-scoped `srchFilter`. Schedule the search Monday 07:00 with throttling per `purpose_id` for 14 days. Export the per-purpose ratio table and the per-event consent_string evidence to ServiceNow GRC for the quarterly DPIA review.
visualization: (1) Single-value tile pair: `purpose_id` count with `art7_1_evidence_gap=yes` (last 7d, red threshold ≥1) and `purpose_id` count with `art7_3_parity_gap=yes` (last 7d, red threshold ≥1). (2) Stacked bar of grants vs withdrawals split by `purpose_id`, time-picker default 30 days, with the per-purpose name joined from a `consent_purposes.csv` lookup. (3) Sortable table — `purpose_id`, `purpose_name`, `grants`, `withdrawals`, `withdrawal_to_grant_ratio`, `grants_missing_consent_string`, `grants_missing_ui_version`, `art7_1_evidence_gap`, `art7_3_parity_gap`; row click drilldowns to the per-event raw search. (4) Timechart (line) of `withdrawal_to_grant_ratio` per `purpose_id` over 90 days — the trace the quarterly DPIA reviewer reads. (5) Drift tile: count of POSTs to `/api/consent` from `access_combined` versus count of `action=grant` events from the CMP, last 7d, drift % — red threshold ≥5% — pointed at CMP webhook availability rather than at Art.7 itself.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Apache Web Server (Splunkbase 3186)
Splunk HTTP Event Collector — Use the HTTP Event Collector
GDPR Article 7 — Conditions for consent (regulator text)
EDPB Guidelines 05/2020 on consent under Regulation 2016/679
IAB Europe Transparency & Consent Framework v2.2 (TCF v2.2 specification)
CNIL deliberation SAN-2022-026 — TikTok €5m cookie-consent fine (January 2023)

knownFalsePositives: This UC reads the CMP's own ledger and the website's URL surface, so the most common false-positives come from gaps between the two, from CMP version churn, and from territorial-scope handling. (a) **TCF version mismatch** — the CMP upgrades from TCF v2.0 to v2.2 mid-window and the regex `^C[A-Za-z0-9_-]{20,}$` plus the `tcf_version=='2.2'` clause flags the in-flight v2.0 events as `art7_1_evidence_gap=yes` even though they were perfectly valid at grant time; pin the regex to the version the CMP was running when the event was minted (use a small lookup keyed on `_time` or on a CMP release tag), not to the current version. (b) **Subject ID rotation on cookie clear** — when a data subject clears cookies the CMP issues a new pseudonymous `subject_id`; the prior grant events become orphaned and the same person appears as multiple subjects; the per-purpose ratio metric is unaffected because it counts events not subjects, but the per-subject withdrawal trace dashboard shows phantom subjects and should be filtered with the CMP's `subject_id_predecessor` field where available. (c) **Geo-conditional consent flows** — users outside the GDPR territorial scope (`gdpr_applies=false`) usually never see the banner and produce no grant events; if your CMP nevertheless logs a synthetic grant for them, the per-purpose grant count looks abnormally high and the withdrawal ratio looks abnormally low, mimicking an Art.7(3) parity gap; the SPL filters with `coalesce(gdpr_applies,'true')=='true'` precisely so non-EU traffic is excluded — sanity-check this filter is doing what you expect by comparing the count to your CMP's own EU-only audit log. (d) **CMP webhook outage with backfill** — the CMP buffers events for hours or days during a webhook outage and then floods them in once the webhook recovers, briefly inflating the grant count and dropping the withdrawal ratio in the recovery window; correlate every alert with `index=_internal source=*splunkd_access.log* hec` plus the CMP vendor's own outage log before filing a privacy ticket. (e) **Re-consent campaigns** — when terms of service or the privacy notice changes, every existing user must re-grant under Art.7, producing a spike in `action=grant` events that lasts 14–30 days; whitelist the campaign window via a `consent_campaigns.csv` lookup so the spike is not misread as either organic user growth or a withdrawal-side dark pattern. (f) **A/B test of the consent banner** — `ui_version` churns rapidly during a banner experiment; `grants_missing_ui_version` may stay zero but the `ui_version` distribution per `purpose_id` becomes spiky and the privacy office's `consent_ui_versions.csv` lookup falls behind. Pause the alert for the experiment window OR pre-load every experiment build into the lookup before launch.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `consent`, `web` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.7`. The control objective is: "Per-purpose evidence that every grant carries a parseable TCF v2.2 consent_string and ui_version (Art.7(1) demonstration), and that the rolling withdrawal-to-grant ratio sits inside the privacy-office-signed band evidencing withdrawal-as-easy-as-grant parity (Art.7(3))." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `consent`, `web` under `cmp:tcf:event`, `access_combined`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


One operational consideration shapes the deployment of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Stand up a dedicated `consent` index, mint a HEC token scoped to it, point the CMP webhook (OneTrust / TrustArc / Didomi / Cookiebot / Iubenda) at the HEC endpoint with the TCF v2.2 payload enabled, and deploy `Splunk_TA_apache` to the web tier so consent endpoint hits land in `index=web`. Restrict both indexes to a `privacy_office` role using `srchIndexesAllowed` and a purpose-scoped `srchFilter`. Schedule the search Monday 07:00 with throttling per `purpose_id` for 14 days. Export the per-purpose ratio table and the per-event consent_string evidence to ServiceNow GRC for the quarterly DPIA review. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

The detection fires when `grants > 0`. For "GDPR Consent Management Audit Trail," the aggregation is the preference-management evidence — each row represents a data subject's current consent state, and drift from the authoritative preference store is the finding. This threshold is the operational heart of the control — set it too low and the signal drowns in noise; set it too high and real compliance deviations go undetected. The value `0` should never be treated as universal; it must be calibrated against your specific operational baseline.

Calibration procedure: before enabling this as a production alert, run the base search over the last 30 days with the threshold removed and compute distribution statistics:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats count by grants
| stats avg(grants) as mean, perc50(grants) as p50, perc90(grants) as p90, perc99(grants) as p99, max(grants) as hi
```

Set the production threshold between p90 and p99 depending on your organisation's alert fatigue tolerance. Document the chosen value in the control specification alongside the date of calibration and the sample size — auditors will ask how you arrived at `0` and the defensible answer is 'data, plus a documented review by the control owner,' not 'the template said so.'

The `count()` aggregation over the last 7 days is a deliberate choice. Compliance reports on sustained behaviour, not instantaneous spikes — a single noisy event crossing the threshold for one second should not page the on-call. If your organisation requires point-in-time sensitivity (e.g. real-time fraud detection), shorten the time window and aggregation span; for accountability reporting, widen them. Tune for the regulation's actual expectation, not engineering preference.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.5: GDPR Consent Management Audit Trail]
cron_schedule = 0 2 * * 1
dispatch.earliest_time = -7d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.5,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=consent sourcetype=cmp:tcf:event earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `action`, `purpose_id`, `tcf_version`, `consent_string`, `ui_version`, `subject_id`, `gdpr_applies`, `method`, `uri`. Run:

```spl
index=consent sourcetype=cmp:tcf:event earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table action, purpose_id, tcf_version, consent_string, ui_version, subject_id, gdpr_applies, method
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `grants`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(grants) as p50 perc90(grants) as p90 perc99(grants) as p99 max(grants) as hi
```

The current threshold `0` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.5: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — (1) Single-Value Tile Pair: `Purpose Id` Count With `Art7 1 Evidence Gap=Yes` (Last 7D, Red Threshold ≥1) And `Purpose Id` Count With `Art7 3 Parity Gap=Yes` (Last 7D, Red Threshold ≥1). (2) Stacked Bar Of Grants Vs Withdrawals Split By `Purpose Id` (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Time-Picker Default 30 Days (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — With The Per-Purpose Name Joined From A `Consent Purposes.Csv` Lookup. (3) Sortable Table — `Purpose Id` (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 4 — `Purpose Name` (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 5 — `Grants` (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 6 — `Withdrawals` (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 7 — `Withdrawal To Grant Ratio` (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 8 — `Grants Missing Consent String` (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 9 — `Grants Missing Ui Version` (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 10 — `Art7 1 Evidence Gap` (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 11 — `Art7 3 Parity Gap`; Row Click Drilldowns To The Per-Event Raw Search. (4) Timechart (Line) Of `Withdrawal To Grant Ratio` Per `Purpose Id` Over 90 Days — The Trace The Quarterly Dpia Reviewer Reads. (5) Drift Tile: Count Of Posts To `/Api/Consent` From `Access Combined` Versus Count Of `Action=Grant` Events From The Cmp (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 12 — Last 7D (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 13 — Drift % — Red Threshold ≥5% — Pointed At Cmp Webhook Availability Rather Than At Art.7 Itself (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Too many or too few alerts:** The threshold is almost certainly miscalibrated for your estate — re-run the percentile calibration (Step 3d) on a representative 30-day window and pick a value between p90 and p99.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We keep an unbroken audit trail of every time someone clicks 'allow' or 'withdraw consent' on the cookie or privacy banner — what they agreed to, what version of the wording they were shown, and when they changed their mind. If the 'withdraw' button stops working as easily as the 'allow' button, the trail tells us before the regulator does.
